title: The Schaghen letter records the 'purchase' of Manhattan
work: The Island at the Center of the World (2004)
take: Shorto stresses that the famous 60-guilders figure survives only in Pieter Schaghen's brief note, and that the Lenape and the Dutch held incompatible ideas of land: the Native people almost certainly understood the exchange as an agreement to share use of the land rather than to surrender it permanently as European property law assumed.

title: First enslaved Africans brought to New Amsterdam
work: In the Shadow of Slavery: African Americans in New York City, 1626-1863 (2003)
take: Harris traces a continuous Black presence in the city to the arrival of enslaved company laborers in the 1620s, arguing that Dutch bondage was comparatively fluid, allowing some to win 'half-freedom' and land grants north of town, and that slavery and later emancipation were central to the formation of class and community in New York.

title: Dutch West India Company is chartered
work: The Colony of New Netherland: A Dutch Settlement in Seventeenth-Century America (2009)
take: Jacobs argues that the Dutch West India Company was internally divided over whether its North American holding should be a colony of trade, a colony of settlement, or a colony at all, a tension that kept New Netherland a commercial trading enterprise far longer than a settler society.

title: Peter Stuyvesant arrives as director-general
work: The Island at the Center of the World (2004)
take: Shorto reframes the late Dutch period as a struggle between the autocratic, intolerant Stuyvesant and the lawyer Adriaen van der Donck, whose push for representative government and Dutch-style tolerance Shorto credits with seeding a pluralist, free-trading character that shaped New York and America.

title: First Jewish refugees reach New Amsterdam
who: Edwin G. Burrows and Mike Wallace
work: Gotham: A History of New York City to 1898 (1998)
take: Burrows and Wallace recount that Stuyvesant sought to expel the 23 Sephardic refugees but was overruled by the Dutch West India Company, whose commercial priorities and Jewish investors led it to grant them the right to remain, an early instance of profit-driven pragmatism trumping the director's intolerance.

title: The Flushing Remonstrance defends religious liberty
who: Edwin G. Burrows and Mike Wallace
work: Gotham: A History of New York City to 1898 (1998)
take: Burrows and Wallace treat the 1657 Remonstrance and John Bowne's later appeal as a landmark assertion of liberty of conscience, situating New Amsterdam's contested tolerance within the commercial pluralism the Dutch fostered rather than crediting it to modern ideals of religious freedom.

title: The English seize New Amsterdam, renaming it New York
work: The Island at the Center of the World (2004)
take: Shorto argues that the 1664 surrender did not erase the Dutch legacy: the generous Articles of Capitulation preserved a diverse, mercantile, comparatively tolerant society whose imprint on New York and on American ideas of pluralism far outlasted the 40 years of formal Dutch rule.

title: The Bolting Act hands the city a flour monopoly
who: Edwin G. Burrows and Mike Wallace
work: Gotham: A History of New York City to 1898 (1998)
take: Burrows and Wallace present the 1678 bolting monopoly as an engine of early growth, arguing that the city's exclusive right to bolt flour and pack it for export drove a boom in milling, shipping and population that helped establish New York as a leading colonial commercial port.

title: Leisler's Rebellion seizes control of the colony
who: Edwin G. Burrows and Mike Wallace
work: Gotham: A History of New York City to 1898 (1998)
take: Burrows and Wallace read Leisler's 1689 seizure of power as an eruption of class and ethnic resentment by ordinary Dutch and artisan New Yorkers against a wealthy Anglo-Dutch merchant elite, a rift whose bitterness poisoned city and colonial politics for decades after his execution.

title: The 1712 slave revolt
work: Black and White Manhattan: The History of Racial Formation in Colonial New York City (2004)
take: Foote situates the 1712 uprising within the ongoing construction of racial categories in colonial Manhattan, arguing that white authorities' violent response and the harsher slave codes that followed were part of how a racial order was built and enforced in the city.

title: The Zenger trial and freedom of the press
work: Considering Zenger: Partisan Politics and the Legal Profession in Provincial New York (Columbia Law Review, 1994)
take: Moglen argues that the Zenger acquittal is better understood as an episode of factional partisan politics and lawyerly maneuvering in provincial New York than as a deliberate constitutional milestone, cautioning against the later mythology that made it a straightforward founding of press freedom.

title: The 1741 conspiracy trials
work: New York Burning: Liberty, Slavery, and Conspiracy in Eighteenth-Century Manhattan (2005)
take: Lepore argues that Justice Daniel Horsmanden's citywide slave-conspiracy scenario was almost certainly a fabrication born of panic, and that fears of slave plots functioned as a kind of shadow politics, revealing how slavery both destabilized and helped create colonial New York's political order.

title: The prison ships of Wallabout Bay
work: Forgotten Patriots: The Untold Story of American Prisoners During the Revolutionary War (2008)
take: Burrows argues that far more Americans died in British captivity, especially on the Wallabout Bay prison hulks, than on all the war's battlefields combined, and that modern historians long underestimated this suffering by wrongly dismissing survivors' accounts as exaggerated propaganda.

title: The Battle of Long Island
who: Edwin G. Burrows and Mike Wallace
work: Gotham: A History of New York City to 1898 (1998)
take: Burrows and Wallace frame the 1776 campaign as a catastrophe narrowly survived: the British flanking victory on Long Island and the nighttime evacuation across the East River saved the Continental Army but handed Britain the city, which became its North American headquarters for the rest of the war.

title: The statue of King George III is toppled
who: Edwin G. Burrows and Mike Wallace
work: Gotham: A History of New York City to 1898 (1998)
take: Burrows and Wallace describe the toppling of the King George III statue at Bowling Green as a charged act of popular, crowd-driven revolutionary iconoclasm, its lead melted into musket balls, that expressed the militant street politics of New York's Sons of Liberty.
